Preprint.
Beyond the Illusion of Consensus: From Surface Heuristics to
Knowledge-Grounded Evaluation in LLM-as-a-Judge
Mingyang Song, Mao Zheng, Chenning Xu
Large Language Model Department
Tencent, China
nickmysong@tencent.com
Abstract
The paradigm of LLM-as-a-judge relies on a critical assumption, namely
that high inter-evaluator agreement indicates reliable and objective evalua-
tion. We present two complementary findings that challenge this assump-
tion.First, we demonstrate that this consensus is frequently illusory. We
identify and formalizeEvaluation Illusion, a phenomenon where LLM
judges generate sophisticated critiques yet anchor scores on shared surface
heuristics rather than substantive quality. Through a large-scale study of
105,600 evaluation instances (32 LLMs ×3 frontier judges ×100 tasks ×11
temperatures), we show that model-level agreement (Spearman ρ= 0.99)
masks fragile sample-level agreement (Pearson ¯r= 0.72; absolute agree-
ment ICC =0.67), that merely sharing rubric structure restores 62% of
total agreement, and that high-quality outputs paradoxically receive the
leastconsistent evaluations.Second, we demonstrate that dynamically
generating evaluation rubrics grounded in domain knowledge produces
more meaningful assessment. We introduce MERG (Metacognitive En-
hanced Rubric Generation), a knowledge-driven rubric generation frame-
work whose domain-selective effects confirm this. Agreementincreasesin
codified domains (Education +22%, Academic +27%) where knowledge
anchors evaluators on shared standards, while it decreases in subjective
domains where genuine evaluative pluralism emerges. These findings
suggest that evaluation rubrics should be dynamically enriched with expert
knowledge rather than relying on generic criteria, with implications for
reward modeling in RLAIF.
1 Introduction
Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as automated evaluators of
text quality, replacing or supplementing human judgment at scale. The LLM-as-a-Judge
paradigm (Zheng et al., 2023; Chiang et al., 2024), together with Reinforcement Learning
from AI Feedback (RLAIF; Lee et al., 2023), now underpins model ranking, reward model
training, and alignment pipelines across the field. Early empirical results were encourag-
ing. GPT-4 achieved over 80% agreement with human preferences on MT-Bench (Zheng
et al., 2023), G-Eval attained a Spearman correlation of 0.514 with human judgments on
summarization (Liu et al., 2023), and AlpacaEval rankings closely tracked Chatbot Arena’s
crowd-sourced preferences (Li et al., 2023; Chiang et al., 2024). These results established a
credible foundation for automated assessment.
Building on this foundation, rubric-based evaluation protocols have matured rapidly, de-
composing quality into structured dimensions with explicit scoring criteria (Ye et al., 2024;
Kim et al., 2024a;b; Rao & Callison-Burch, 2026). WritingBench (Wu et al., 2025) exemplifies
the latest advances by dynamically generating query-dependent criteria tailored to each
writing task, achieving 87% human agreement through its adaptive evaluation framework.
Meanwhile, the capabilities of LLM-based judges have themselves become an active research
subject (Li et al., 2024a; Tan et al., 2025), and automated evaluation has been further scaled
to power leaderboards and preference pipelines (Li et al., 2024b). Together, stronger models

Preprint.
and more principled instruments have made automated evaluation appear increasingly
trustworthy, and a natural assumption has taken hold across the field, namely that when
frontier evaluators independently converge on nearly identical scores, the consensus must
reflect a shared, substantive understanding of quality.
In this paper, we present large-scale empirical evidence that this inference is often wrong.
Frontier evaluators frequently anchor their judgments on shared surface heuristics (for-
matting, fluency, confident tone, and structural polish) rather than substantive quality,
producing what we termEvaluation Illusion. When multiple evaluators default to the same
heuristic repertoire, they create aShared Illusion, a statistically robust yet epistemically
shallow consensus. As a concrete illustration (Figure 1), frontier evaluators independently
award scores above 9.0 to a pitch deck for a for-profit K-12 tutoring startup in China, praising
its “masterful formatting” and “persuasive projections,” yet unanimously miss that China’s
2021 “Double Reduction” policy banned this business model entirely. The agreement is real;
the understanding is not.
To test whether baseline consensus reflects genuine deliberation or shared heuristics, we
introduceMERG(Metacognitive Enhanced Rubric Generation), a four-stage framework
that requires evaluators to articulate task-relevant domain knowledge and surface their own
biasesbeforegenerating a scoring rubric. Drawing on the distinction between System 1 (fast,
heuristic-driven) and System 2 (slow, knowledge-grounded) processing (Kahneman, 2011),
MERG serves a dual purpose, both as adiagnostic probethat tests whether knowledge injec-
tion preserves consensus (indicating genuine deliberation) or disrupts it (indicating heuristic
reliance), and as aprescriptive toolthat yields more substantive, knowledge-grounded assess-
ments.
We deploy MERG at unprecedented scale, evaluating 32 LLMs spanning Base, Instruct,
and Thinking tiers ×3 frontier judges (Claude 4.5 Opus, Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-5.1) ×100
diverse writing tasks ×11 temperature settings, yielding 105,600 evaluation instances. Our
experiments reveal three principal findings.
1.Knowledge injection deconstructs the Shared Illusion.MERG reduces inter-evaluator
agreement by 21 to 34% (Cohen’s d= 0.97 to 1.42; Table 3). The effect is domain-selective,
with agreementincreasingin codified domains (Education ∆K= + 0.22; Academic +0.27),
where knowledge anchors evaluators on shared professional standards, butdecreasing
in subjective ones (Literature −0.06), where it surfaces genuine evaluative pluralism.
This asymmetry rules out the noise hypothesis and confirms that baseline consensus is
heuristic-driven.
2.The Rubric Commensurability Problem(§3.3). When evaluators generate rubrics inde-
pendently, agreement collapses to near-random levels ( ¯r≈0.24). Merely sharing rubric
dimension names, without any content or knowledge, restores 62% of total agreement.
Much of what passes for evaluator reliability in the literature may be an artifact of shared
evaluation instruments rather than genuine shared judgment.
3.The Resolution Paradox(§3.2). Model-level Spearman ρ≥ 0.984 (at t=0.0) coexists with
sample-level Pearson ¯r=0.72 (averaged across 32 models ×11 temperatures), a gap of
0.27. Leaderboards validate evaluators at the macro-resolution where the system reliably
distinguishes coarse quality tiers (Base vs. Thinking, d= 2.58), but RLAIF deploys them
at per-sample micro-resolution, the very granularity where the illusion is strongest and
fine-grained signals are unreliable.
These findings carry a troubling implication. Output quality and evaluator agreement are
negativelycorrelated (Spearman ρ=− 0.513, p= 0.003, across 32 models at t=0.0). Base
models elicit ¯r=0.81 while Thinking models elicit only ¯r=0.76 (sample-level Pearson rat
t=0.0). Surface features suffice for judging low-quality outputs, but high-quality outputs
push evaluators into the heuristic zone where the illusion is strongest and where RLAIF
reward signals most need to discriminate. Preliminary experiments further indicate that
reward models trained on MERG-grounded preferences resist overoptimization three times
longer than those trained on baseline preferences (§4).
Our contributions are threefold.(1)We formalizeEvaluation Illusionand theShared
Illusion, introduce the knowledge-grounding diagnostic ∆K, and demonstrate through

Figure 1:Illustration of Evaluation Illusion.Without knowledge grounding, evaluators form a
“Shared Illusion,” unanimously rewarding the professional formatting of a fundamentally flawed
business pitch. MERG forces knowledge activation, revealing that Claude penalizes the regulatory
violation while Gemini continues to reward surface heuristics. GPT (3.7, not shown) penalizes even

105,600 instances that baseline evaluator consensus is largely heuristic-driven ( ∆K=− 0.22
to+0.27, d≥ 0.97).(2)We identify two structural inflation mechanisms that explain why
this illusion persists undetected, namely theRubric Commensurability Problem(62% of
agreement from rubric structure alone) and theResolution Paradox(model-level ρ= 0.99
vs. sample-level ¯r=0.72 vs. absolute ICC= 0.67).(3)We introduce MERG as a practical
framework for knowledge-grounded rubric generation and provide preliminary evidence
that it mitigates reward overoptimization in RLAIF.
2 Methodology
2.1 Experimental Design
Data.We sample 100 prompts from WritingBench (Wu et al., 2025) via stratified sampling,
covering 12 to 19 prompts per domain across 94 out of 100 subdomains, balanced between
50 English and 50 Chinese tasks. WritingBench spans six domains (Literature, Education,
Academic, Finance, Politics, and Mixed), providing broad coverage of writing genres from
creative fiction to technical reports.
Models.We evaluate 32 LLMs spanning three capability tiers to ensure coverage of the
full quality spectrum.
•BASE(8)includes Qwen2.5-{7B,32B}, Qwen3-{4B,8B,14B,30B-A3B}, Llama-3.1-8B, and
Llama-4-Scout, all raw pretrained models without instruction tuning.
•INSTRUCT(13)includes Qwen2.5-{7B,32B,72B}, Qwen3-{4B,8B,14B,30B-A3B,32B,235B-
A22B}, DeepSeek-V3, InternLM3-8B, Llama-3.1-8B, and Llama-4-Scout, all instruction-
tuned variants.
•THINKING(11)includes DeepSeek-R1-0528, R1-Qwen3-8B, R1-Distill-{Llama-8B,Qwen-
7B,Qwen-32B}, and Qwen3-{4B,8B,14B,30B-A3B,32B,235B-A22B}, all models trained with
chain-of-thought reinforcement learning.
3

Preprint.
This three-tier design is intentional. Base models produce outputs with obvious quality
differences (e.g., incoherent text), enabling a calibration check where high genuine evaluator
agreement is expected. Thinking models occupy the high-quality region where Evaluation
Illusion is hypothesized to manifest most strongly.
Evaluators.We employ three frontier LLMs as evaluators, namelyClaude 4.5 Opus,
Gemini 2.5 Pro, andGPT-5.1. Using three diverse commercial models from different
providers ensures that any observed agreement cannot be attributed to shared training data
or architectures. The baseline uses WritingBench’s checklist-based rubric with 1 to 10 integer
scoring. Each of the 32 models’ outputs (100 prompts each) is evaluated by all 3 evaluators
at 11 temperatures ( t∈ { 0.0, 0.1, . . ., 1.0}), yielding a total of 32 ×100×3×11=105,600
evaluation instances.
Metrics.We measure agreement at three granularities.
•Sample-levelagreement is measured by Pearson correlation rbetween evaluator pairs
per model ×temperature cell. When computed over per-sample mean scores (averaging
across the 5 rubric criteria per prompt, yielding n≈ 100 data points), this is used for
per-model agreement ranking (Tables 4 and 5). When computed over per-criterion scores
(n≈500 data points), this is used for cross-temperature analyses (§3.2).
•Cell-levelabsolute agreement is measured by Intraclass Correlation Coefficient ICC(2,1), a
two-way random-effects, single-measures, absolute-agreement metric (Shrout & Fleiss,
1979) computed per model ×temperature cell. Unlike Pearson r, ICC penalizes systematic
scoring biases between evaluators.
•Model-levelagreement is measured by Spearman rank correlation ρacross models, com-
puted from mean scores per model. This captures ranking consistency.
All pairwise differences are tested with paired t-tests (Bonferroni-corrected, α= 0.05/k);
effect sizes are reported via Cohen’s d. To quantify the presence of Evaluation Illusion, we
define theKnowledge-Grounding Diagnostic(∆ K) as (Eq. 1):
∆K=¯rMERG−¯rBaseline (1)
where ∆K<0 indicates that baseline agreement was likely a Shared Illusion deconstructed
by knowledge injection.
2.2 Metacognitive Enhanced Rubric Generation
To test whether baseline evaluator agreement reflects genuine deliberation or heuristic-
driven Evaluation Illusion, we require a controlled intervention. We designMERG(Metacog-
nitive Enhanced Rubric Generation), a four-stage framework that forces LLM evaluators
to transition from System 1 (fast, heuristic-driven pattern matching) to System 2 (slow,
knowledge-grounded deliberation) before assigning a score (Table 1). MERG serves a dual
purpose, both as adiagnostic probethat reveals the depth of baseline consensus and as a
prescriptive frameworkthat demonstrates how dynamically injecting domain knowledge into
rubric generation produces more substantive evaluation.
Stage 1: Knowledge Activation.Before examining any output, the evaluator is forced to
articulate domain-specific knowledge relevant to the task. This includes genre conventions
(e.g., noir fiction tropes), quality benchmarks (e.g., peer-reviewed standards for academic
abstracts), and common pitfalls. This stage serves as the primary grounding mechanism,
anchoring subsequent evaluation in explicit domain expertise rather than implicit surface
heuristics.
Stage 2: Metacognitive Reflection.The evaluator identifies potential heuristics or biases
it might default to (e.g., being swayed by confident tone or professional formatting) and
articulates mitigation strategies. This explicitly operationalizes bias awareness (Wang et al.,
2023; Wataoka et al., 2024) to prevent the evaluator from falling back into heuristic scoring
patterns.

Stage 3: Dynamic Rubric Generation.The evaluator synthesizes the activated knowledge
into a task-specific rubric R. Unlike static rubrics that encourage generic scoring, each task
receives unique, deep dimensions. For example, a Gothic horror task generates “Atmo-
spheric Dread Construction” and “The Uncanny Familiar” instead of generic “Style” and
“Coherence” (Table 8).
Stage 4: Calibrated Evaluation.The evaluator scores each dimension independently,
citing specific textual evidence. A final bias verification step checks whether any identified
heuristics from Stage 2 influenced the scoring. The overall score is computed as the mean
across dimensions.1
Ablation Variants.To disentangle the sources of evaluator agreement, we design four
controlled ablation variants (Table 2), applied to two representative models (DeepSeek-R1
and Qwen3-235B).
1.Original (4-Stage). Each evaluator independently executes all four MERG stages, gener-
ating its own rubric. This maximizes evaluator independence.
2.5-Dim Per-Dim. All evaluators use a fixed 5-dimension structure (Content, Style, Struc-
ture, Language, Creativity) but generate their own scoring criteria. This isolates the effect
of dimensional standardization.
3.Shared Stages. Claude executes Stages 1 to 3; all evaluators share the resulting rubric
but score independently. This isolates the effect of rubric sharing.
4.Universal. Shared rubrics are precomputed and reused identically across all evaluators
and temperatures. This represents maximal rubric control.
3 Results
3.1 Deconstructing the Shared Illusion
Table 3 presents our central diagnostic finding. Deploying MERG’s knowledge-grounded
evaluation pipelinesystematically reduces inter-evaluator agreementacross all 10 experi-
mental conditions (binomial p< 0.001). For DeepSeek-R1, the mean Pearson correlation
drops by ∆K=− 0.217 ( ¯r= 0.643 →0.426, Cohen’s d= 1.42); for Qwen3-235B, ∆K=− 0.138
(¯r= 0.667 →0.529, d= 0.97). Both effect sizes exceed the conventional “large” threshold of
d= 0.8, indicating a robust and practically significant phenomenon. Figure 2 visualizes this
degradation across the full temperature range.
Interpretation.This reduction serves as empirical evidence for the presence of a Shared
Illusion. Without knowledge grounding, evaluators tend to converge on surface heuris-

tics (fluency, length, formatting, grammatical correctness), producing what appears to be
reliable consensus but is largely heuristic-driven agreement. MERG forces engagement
with substantive, domain-specific criteria (e.g., “Does the noir atmosphere create genuine
psychological tension?” rather than “Is the text well-written?”). This mitigates the heuristic
consensus, revealing genuine disagreements that were previously masked by surface-level
alignment. The agreement did not merely degrade; the evaluation became more substantive.
Corroborating evidence.Two additional observations support this interpretation.
1.Domain selectivity(§3.4). Knowledgeincreasesagreement in codified domains (Edu-
cation, Academic) butdecreasesit in subjective ones (Literature). This asymmetry ( χ2
test, p< 0.01) is inconsistent with a noise hypothesis, which would predict uniform
degradation.
2.Quality and agreement gradient. Across all 32 models at t=0.0, output quality and inter-
evaluator agreement are negatively correlated (Spearman ρ=− 0.513, p= 0.003; Tables 4
and 5). Base models show ¯r= 0.81, Instruct models ¯r= 0.77, and Thinking models
¯r= 0.76 (sample-level Pearson r). This gradient aligns with the Evaluation Illusion
hypothesis, as surface features suffice for distinguishing low-quality outputs (yielding
high genuine agreement), but when evaluating high-quality outputs, the reliance on
heuristics leads to divergent, illusory scoring.

3.2 The Resolution Paradox
Model-Level vs. Sample-Level Agreement.When scores are aggregated to model-level
averages (mean across 100 prompts), evaluator pairs achieve near-perfect rank correlations
att=0.0 (Claude-Gemini ρ= 0.992, Claude-GPT ρ= 0.992, Gemini-GPT ρ= 0.984; all
p< 10−23; grand mean ¯ρ= 0.989). This pattern holds across all 11 temperatures (grand
¯ρ= 0.986, SD =0.007). Yet at the sample level, per-criterion Pearson agreement averaged
across 32 models and 11 temperatures is markedly lower ( ¯r= 0.72), representing a gap
of 0.27 between model-level and sample-level agreement. This discrepancy is systematic.
Claude-GPT shows the highest sample-level agreement ( ¯r=0.77), followed by Gemini-GPT
(0.71) and Claude-Gemini (0.69), indicating divergent evaluation strategies when forced to
make fine-grained judgments (F(2, 93) =14.7,p<0.001).
Validity Check.Before interpreting this gap, we confirm the evaluation system is fun-
damentally sound for coarse distinctions. All three evaluators consistently recover the
expected quality hierarchy, with Base ¯s= 3.05, Instruct ¯s= 5.38, and Thinking ¯s= 5.96
(Cohen’s d= 2.58 for Base vs. Thinking; pairwise ordering accuracy 83.9%). The system is
not broken; rather, Evaluation Illusion isstratified, absent for coarse quality distinctions but
pervasive for fine-grained judgments within a high-quality tier.
The Granularity Boundary.The Resolution Paradox defines the operational limit of LLM
evaluators, where ρmodel≫¯rsample (0.989 at t=0.0 vs. 0.72 across all temperatures). Evalua-
tors agree on which models are better overall (where signal dominates) but exhibit illusory
agreement or disagree substantially on individual outputs (where noise and heuristic biases
dominate). This is precisely the resolution that per-sample RLAIF rewards require. The
paradox suggests that while LLM evaluators are reliable for model-level benchmarking,
their per-sample signals are heavily contaminated by Evaluation Illusion. The stratification
by model type confirms this pattern. Averaged across temperatures, Base models show high

Noise Absorption Across Aggregation Levels.To locate where inter-evaluator noise is
absorbed, we compute a third, intermediate metric, the Intraclass Correlation Coefficient
ICC(2,1), per model ×temperature cell, treating each cell as an n≈100 subject ×3 rater design.
Unlike Pearson r, which captures only linear association, ICC(2,1) penalizesbothranking
disagreementsandsystematic scoring biases (e.g., one evaluator consistently scoring 0.5
points higher). The grand mean ICC(2,1) across all 352 cells (32 models ×11 temperatures)
isICC= 0.67 (SD =0.10),lowerthan the sample-level Pearson ¯r= 0.72. This ordering
(ρmodel =0.989≫¯rsample =0.72>ICC= 0.67) reveals that evaluator consensus degrades
through two distinct mechanisms. First, ranking noise at the sample level ( ρ→r , a gap of
0.27), and second, systematic inter-evaluator scoring biases ( r→ICC , a further gap of 0.05).
The ICC stratifies by model type in the expected direction, with Base models showing ICC
=0.75, Instruct =0.65, and Thinking =0.63 (at t=0.0), with the two highest-quality models
(DeepSeek-R1-0528, ICC =0.38; Qwen3-235B-Thinking, ICC =0.52) exhibiting the lowest
absolute agreement. This three-level decomposition confirms that model-level agreement
isdoublyinflated relative to per-sample signal quality, making it a poor proxy for RLAIF
reward reliability.
3.3 The Rubric Commensurability Problem
If baseline agreement is a Shared Illusion, what sustains it, and what can knowledge-
grounded rubric generation teach us about moving beyond it? Table 6 decomposes the
structural sources of evaluator agreement through controlled ablation, revealing that the
illusion is heavily scaffolded by the evaluation instrument itself.
•Independent rubrics (Original). When each evaluator generates its own knowledge-
grounded rubric independently through MERG, the Shared Illusion completely breaks
down ( ¯r= 0.224 for DeepSeek-R1, 0.254 for Qwen3-235B). This near-zero agreement
demonstrates that without structural constraints, evaluators construct fundamentally
different, valid evaluation frameworks.
•Dimensional standardization (5-Dim). Simply sharing dimensionnames, without sharing
any actual rubric content or knowledge, more than doubles agreement ( ∆r= + 0.32
for DeepSeek-R1, +0.45 for Qwen3-235B; 2.4 to 2.8 ×). This artificial constraint forces
evaluators back into a shared heuristic space.
•Rubric sharing (Shared). Having Claude generate the full rubric while other evaluators
only score yields mixed effects ( ∆r= + 0.06 for DeepSeek-R1 but −0.05 for Qwen3-235B),
suggesting that beyond dimensional structure, additional rubric specificity has limited
and model-dependent impact.

Qualitative Evidence.Table 8 contrasts rubric dimensions generated by WritingBench

More importantly, qualitative analysis of the maximum-disagreement cases under MERG
reveals exactlywhyagreement decreases. Knowledge injection transforms some evaluators
into domain experts while others remain surface-level readers. We observe three recurring
patterns of deep disagreement.
•Logical Verification vs. Formatting.In an investment pitch deck task (Sample #501),
Gemini awarded a near-perfect 9.9, praising the “masterful understanding” and excellent
formatting. However, Claude (6.5) and GPT (3.7) utilized their activated business knowl-
edge to audit the content, discovering that the financial projections were mathematically
inconsistent (ARPU calculations failed to match claimed totals) and the strategy fatally
ignored critical Chinese regulatory context (the “Double Reduction” policy for EdTech).
•Concept Auditing vs. Keyword Matching.In a technical patent disclosure task (Sample
#45), Gemini again gave a 10.0 for “credible and specific details.” Conversely, GPT
(3.2) and Claude (6.4) detected a fundamental misuse of Zero-Knowledge Proof (ZKP)
cryptographic concepts and noted that the implementation details were hallucinated
rather than grounded in the source paper.
•Substance vs. Outline.In a creative writing task (Sample #739), Gemini awarded 9.7
for “exceptional thematic synthesis.” Claude (3.0), however, recognized that the model
had merely generated a sophisticated structural outline rather than actually writing the
requested narrative prose.
Across all these cases, high baseline agreement arises because evaluators default to a shared
heuristic repertoire, rewarding professional formatting, confident tone, and appropriate

specific constraints. Agreement drops not because evaluation becomes noisier, but because
detecting deep flaws is inherently more difficult, and more evaluator-dependent, than
recognizing surface polish.
4 Discussion
The Two Faces of Evaluation Illusion.Our findings establish two complementary claims.
First, the consensus among LLM evaluators is structurally fragile. The Resolution Paradox
(ρmodel =0.989≫¯rsample =0.72>ICC= 0.67), the Rubric Commensurability Problem
(62% of agreement attributable to rubric structure alone), and domain-dependent sensitivity
collectively demonstrate that high inter-evaluator agreement often reflects shared evaluation
instruments and surface heuristics rather than genuine convergent judgment.Second, this
fragility is not cause for despair but an opportunity. By dynamically enriching evaluation
rubrics with domain knowledge through MERG, we show that evaluation can be made more
substantive. The 21 to 34% drop in agreement is not evaluation failure but a recalibration
toward depth. This parallels human annotation, where agreement on “easy” items coexists
with systematic disagreement on consequential ones (Plank, 2022). We extend this to LLM
evaluation with a key distinction. The tradeoff isdomain-dependent(codified domains
maintain agreement; subjective ones decrease it),measurablevia ∆K, and most acute in the
high-quality region where alignment matters most.
Implications for Reward Modeling.Prior work documents reward overoptimization (Gao
et al., 2023; Rafailov et al., 2024) but leaves its source unclear. Our findings suggest a potential
upstream explanation, namely that the reward signal itself may constitute a Shared Illusion.
To test this, we train two DPO reward models (Rafailov et al., 2023) using Llama-3-8B-
Instruct on WritingBench, one from Baseline preferences and one from MERG preferences.
The Baseline RM overoptimizes rapidly (true quality peaks at step 400, then declines), while
the MERG RM maintains alignment up to step 1200. The Resolution Paradox compounds
this problem. Researchers validate evaluators at the model level ( ρ≥ 0.984) but deploy
them for per-sample rewards where r≈0.72 and absolute agreement is even lower (ICC
=0.67), a granularity mismatch that Evaluation Illusion exploits.
Structural vs. Substantive Agreement.The ablation quantifies a distinction implicit in
prior work betweenstructuralagreement (from shared instruments) andsubstantiveagree-
ment (from genuine convergence). Rubric structure alone accounts for 62% of total agree-
ment ( ¯r= 0.24 →0.62). This has a direct practical implication. Any evaluation pipeline
reporting high agreement without controlling for rubric similarity is conflating two funda-
mentally different sources of consensus.
Practical Recommendations.(1)Audit agreement depthby testing whether consensus
survives knowledge injection via ∆K; flag signals where |∆K|>0.15. (2)Match depth to
domainby using knowledge-grounded evaluation for codified domains; accept irreducible
disagreement in subjective ones. (3)Diversify RLAIF signalsby aggregating rewards
across multiple rubric structures to reduce structural bias. (4)Mind the resolution gap.
Model-level ρ(0.989), sample-level r(0.72), and absolute ICC (0.67) measure increasingly
stringent agreement; validate at the granularity you deploy. MERG’s overhead ( ∼3−4×
tokens) is amortizable because Stages 1 to 3 are prompt-dependent and cacheable, reducing
marginal cost to∼1.3×in production pipelines.
Toward Knowledge-Driven Evaluation Rubrics.Our findings motivate a broader method-
ological recommendation. Current evaluation pipelines typically apply generic, static
rubrics across diverse tasks, inadvertently encouraging the surface-heuristic convergence
that produces Evaluation Illusion. We propose that evaluation rubrics should bedynami-
cally generatedwith explicit domain knowledge injection. MERG instantiates this principle
through its four-stage pipeline, but the core insight generalizes beyond our specific im-
plementation. Any evaluation framework that forces evaluators to articulate and apply

task-relevant expert knowledge before scoring will produce assessments that are more
substantive, more domain-sensitive, and ultimately more useful as training signals for
alignment.
5 Related Work
LLM Evaluation and Its Limitations.The paradigm of using LLMs as evaluators (Zheng
et al., 2023) underpins leaderboards (Chiang et al., 2024), benchmarks (Li et al., 2023; 2024b),
chain-of-thought evaluation (Liu et al., 2023), and fine-tuned judges (Zhu et al., 2025);
see Li et al. (2024a) for a comprehensive survey. These works uniformly report aggregate
inter-evaluator agreement as evidence of reliability, often without examining whether that
agreement reflects surface-level or substantive convergence. Recent studies have begun
to question this assumption, noting that high agreement can sometimes mask underlying
evaluation flaws (Wang et al., 2023). Documented biases in LLM evaluators include ver-
bosity (Zheng et al., 2023), position (Wang et al., 2023), self-enhancement (Wataoka et al.,
2024), format (Li et al., 2024a), and contextual biases (Li et al., 2025). JudgeBench (Tan et al.,
2025) reveals that even frontier models perform marginally above random on challenging
evaluation tasks. Our work provides a systematic test of this distinction, introducing the
concept of Evaluation Illusion and demonstrating that shared biases across evaluators
produce consensus not because they converge on quality but because they share the same
preference for surface heuristics.
Rubric-Based and Checklist Evaluation.Structured evaluation approaches aim to im-
prove reliability by providing explicit criteria. Methods include Prometheus (Kim et al.,
2024a;b), OpenRubrics (Liu et al., 2025), FLASK (Ye et al., 2024), SedarEval (Fan et al., 2025),
and domain-specific rubrics (Pathak et al., 2025). Recent frameworks like AutoRubric (Rao
& Callison-Burch, 2026) further automate the generation of evaluation criteria. Checklist
methods decompose evaluation into binary items. CheckEval (Lee et al., 2025) reduces
evaluator variance, while Zhang et al. (2026) uses checklist rewards for RL training and Li
et al. (2024c) propose split-and-merge strategies for calibrating position bias in pairwise
evaluation. Rezaei et al. (2025) elicit rubrics from pairwise comparisons; LitBench (Fein et al.,
2025) provides debiased human annotations for creative writing. While these methods aim
toimproveevaluation quality, we use rubric variation as adiagnosticto expose Evaluation
Illusion, demonstrating that shared rubric structure can artificially inflate agreement.
Alignment, Metacognition, and Annotation Disagreement.Gao et al. (2023) established
scaling laws for reward overoptimization, and Rafailov et al. (2024) extended the analysis to
direct alignment algorithms like DPO (Rafailov et al., 2023). Recent work on Self-Rewarding
Language Models (Yuan et al., 2024) explores using the model itself as a critic during
training, highlighting the critical role of the evaluator’s signal quality. These works treat
the divergence as emergingduringoptimization; our findings provide a complementary
upstream explanation, suggesting that the reward signal may already be hallucinating
before optimization begins. On the metacognitive front, Self-Refine (Madaan et al., 2023) and
Reflexion (Shinn et al., 2023) demonstrate that iterative self-reflection improves generation
quality; MENTOR (Shan et al., 2025) applies metacognition-driven self-evolution to uncover
implicit domain risks. While these methods use metacognition to improvegeneration,
MERG applies it toevaluation, using knowledge grounding as a probe to deconstruct
Evaluation Illusion. Finally, Plank (2022) argues that disagreement in human annotations is
not merely noise but carries meaningful signal about item difficulty and ambiguity. Our
work demonstrates the converse in the LLM setting, showing thatagreementcan itself be
noise, a Shared Illusion driven by heuristics rather than shared understanding.
6 Conclusion
Through 105,600 evaluation instances spanning 32 models, 3 frontier evaluators, 100 writing
tasks, and 11 temperature settings, we establish two complementary findings.First, we
identify and formalizeEvaluation Illusion, the phenomenon where LLM judges output
12

Preprint.
sophisticated critiques but base their actual scores on shared surface heuristics. Model-level
agreement (Spearman ρ= 0.99) masks fragile sample-level agreement (Pearson ¯r=0.72;
absolute agreement ICC =0.67), and merely sharing rubric structure accounts for 62% of
total consensus.Second, we demonstrate that dynamically generating evaluation rubrics
grounded in domain knowledge through MERG produces more calibrated assessment
(∆K=− 0.14 to −0.22, Cohen’s d= 0.97 to 1.42). Domain-dependent patterns, where
knowledgeincreasesagreement in codified domains butdecreasesit in subjective ones,
rule out the noise hypothesis and confirm that baseline agreement is largely driven by
convergence on surface features.
Combined with theResolution Paradox( ρmodel =0.989 ≫¯rsample =0.72>ICC=
0.67) and theRubric Commensurability Problem(where structure accounts for 62% of
agreement), our findings suggest that LLM evaluation is valid but shallow. The system
correctly identifies which models are better overall but suffers from Evaluation Illusion
at the per-sample granularity that RLAIF reward signals require. By demonstrating that
knowledge-grounded rubric generation recalibrates this evaluation toward substance, we
offer not only a diagnosis but a practical path forward.
These results carry direct implications for the alignment pipeline, as reward models trained
on evaluator agreement may be optimizing against a Shared Illusion. Our work suggests a
constructive path forward. Evaluation rubrics should not be static, generic instruments but
should be dynamically enriched with task-relevant expert knowledge. MERG instantiates
this principle, and its domain-selective effects confirm its value. We recommend that
the community treat agreement as a necessary but insufficient condition for reliability,
distinguish structural from substantive consensus, and adopt knowledge-grounded rubric
generation as standard practice in evaluation pipelines.
Limitations
Our study has several limitations. First, our evaluation is conducted on creative and
professional writing tasks via WritingBench; the generalizability of Evaluation Illusion to
exact-answer domains (e.g., code generation, mathematical reasoning) warrants further
investigation. Second, all three evaluators are proprietary frontier models (Claude, Gemini,
GPT); the findings may not transfer to open-source or fine-tuned judge models (Zhu et al.,
2025). Third, we lack human ground-truth annotations and therefore cannot claim which
evaluation method is absolutely moreaccurate; only that knowledge injection deconstructs
the illusion of agreement. Fourth, the full MERG ablation was limited to two representative
models (DeepSeek-R1 and Qwen3-235B) due to computational cost; a broader deployment
across all 32 models would strengthen generalizability claims. Fifth, the RLAIF downstream
experiment is preliminary, using a single policy model (Llama-3-8B-Instruct) on a subset of
tasks; larger-scale alignment experiments are needed to confirm the observed mitigation of
reward overoptimization. Finally, deeper evaluation is not necessarily more agreed-upon;
the irreducible disagreement in subjective domains (Literature ∆K=− 0.06) reflects genuine
differences in aesthetic judgment, not evaluation failure.
Ethics Statement
This work evaluates the reliability of LLM-based evaluation systems and does not involve
human subjects or personally identifiable information. All evaluated model outputs are
generated from the publicly available WritingBench benchmark. We use proprietary LLM
APIs (Claude, Gemini, GPT) under their standard terms of service. Our findings highlight
potential risks in over-relying on automated evaluation for alignment, which we believe
serves the broader goal of responsible AI development. We acknowledge that the concept
of “Evaluation Illusion” should not be used to dismiss LLM evaluation entirely, but rather
to encourage more rigorous evaluation practices. The illustrative example in Figure 1 uses
real evaluation scores from our experiments (Sample #501) applied to an LLM-generated
pitch deck, not a real business plan.

We provide the core prompt templates for each MERG stage. Variables in {braces} are filled
at runtime.
Stage 1: Knowledge Activation.
You are an expert evaluator. Before evaluating,
activate your domain knowledge.
Task Type: {task_type}
Original Query: {query}
Systematically activate knowledge in:
1. Task Type Identification
2. Genre-Specific Quality Standards
3. Domain Knowledge Requirements
4. Evaluation Challenges
5. Quality Indicators (excellent/good/poor)
Return as structured JSON.
Stage 2: Metacognitive Reflection.
Reflect on your evaluation process:
1. Potential Biases: length, format, familiarity,
anchoring, halo effect
2. Blind Spots: what might you overlook?
3. Attention Calibration: what deserves focus?
4. Mitigation Strategies for each bias
Stage 3: Dynamic Rubric Generation.
Using activated knowledge and bias awareness,
generate a task-specific rubric with:
- Exactly 5 dimensions (task-specific, NOT generic)
- For each: name, weight, scoring anchors
(2=poor, 5=adequate, 8=strong, 10=exceptional)
- Each anchor with concrete, observable criteria
Stage 4: Calibrated Evaluation.
Score each rubric dimension independently:
1. Cite specific textual evidence

Case 1: Patent Disclosure (Sample #45).Spread: 6.80(Gemini: 10.0, Claude: 6.4, GPT: 3.2)
Geminipraised “credible and specific details” and “exceptional performance across all
dimensions.”GPT, after knowledge activation, identified that the response misused Zero-
17

Preprint.
Knowledge Proof (ZKP) cryptographic concepts and fabricated implementation details
not grounded in the source paper.Claudedetected structural merit but flagged technical
inaccuracies. This case exemplifiesConcept Auditing vs. Keyword Matching, where Gemini
matched technical keywords without verifying their correctness.
Case 2: Creative Fiction (Sample #739).Spread: 6.70(Gemini: 9.7, GPT: 7.1, Claude: 3.0)
Geminiawarded 9.7 for “exceptional thematic synthesis” and “a masterful integration of
all prompt elements.”Clauderecognized that the model had generated a sophisticated
structural outline rather than the requested narrative prose, noting: “An outline, however
sophisticated, cannot score above 5 to 6 because it fails to deliver the requested deliverable.”
GPTacknowledged the conceptual strength but noted the writing remained at a planning
stage. This case exemplifiesSubstance vs. Outline, where Gemini evaluated theconceptwhile
Claude evaluated thedeliverable.
Case 3: Business Pitch Deck (Sample #501).Spread: 6.20(Gemini: 9.9, Claude: 6.5, GPT:
3.7)
Geminiscored 9.9, praising “a masterful understanding of the core task” and “a strategic
masterpiece.”GPT, after knowledge grounding, identified that the pitch entirely ignored
China’s “Double Reduction” policy (which banned for-profit K-12 tutoring), that financial
projections were internally contradictory (ARPU calculations did not match claimed totals),
and that it represented “piling numbers without building a model.”Claudesimilarly
detected regulatory blindness and placeholder data.
Pattern Summary.Across 100 samples, Gemini scored higher than both other evaluators in
90% of cases (mean 9.0 vs. Claude 7.1, GPT 6.9). The mean pairwise absolute difference was
Claude/Gemini 2.32, Claude/GPT 1.10, Gemini/GPT 2.34. Of 100 samples, 78% showed a
score spread >2 points, 41% >3 points, and 19% >4 points. This systematic pattern suggests
that Gemini is most susceptible to Evaluation Illusion under MERG, consistently rewarding
surface presentation even after knowledge activation.
D Sample-Level Agreement
Figure 5 presents the complete sample-level agreement landscape across all 32 evaluated
models at 11 temperature settings ( t∈ { 0.0, 0.1, . . ., 1.0}). Four key observations emerge
from this comprehensive view:
Temperature Invariance.Across all models, sample-level Pearson rremains remarkably
stable as temperature varies. This rules out a simple explanation that disagreement arises
from stochastic variation in evaluator outputs; even at t=0.0 (fully deterministic), agreement
rarely exceeds r= 0.80. The flatness of the curves indicates that Evaluation Illusion is a
structuralproperty of how LLM evaluators process writing quality, not a consequence of
sampling noise.
Pair-Specific Hierarchy.A consistent hierarchy emerges across models, with Claude/GPT
agreement generally highest ( ¯r≈ 0.77), followed by Gemini/GPT ( ¯r≈ 0.71) and
Claude/Gemini ( ¯r≈ 0.69). This pattern is stable across Base, Instruct, and Thinking
model tiers, suggesting that Claude and GPT share more similar internal evaluation heuris-
tics, while Gemini employs a meaningfully different scoring strategy, consistent with the
finding that Gemini is most susceptible to surface-level heuristic scoring (§3.1).
Model-Type Effects.Base models (light blue shading) tend to showhighersample-level
agreement ( ¯r≈ 0.81 at t=0.0) compared to Thinking models (dark shading, ¯r≈ 0.76).
This inverse relationship between model quality and evaluator agreement supports the
Resolution Paradox. Lower-quality outputs produce more obviously identifiable flaws that
evaluators converge on, while high-quality outputs push evaluation into the ambiguous
region where heuristic divergence dominates.

The pattern is consistent: agreement is moderate ( r≈0.5 to 0.8) and largely temperature-invariant,
confirming that the Resolution Paradox (high model-level ρ, low sample-level r) is a universal property
of LLM evaluation, not a model-specific artifact.
Outlier Models.Several models exhibit notably lower agreement: DeepSeek-R1-0528-

E Case Studies Under MERG
This section presents representative case studies illustrating how knowledge injection
reshapes evaluator behavior. All cases are drawn from the MERG evaluation of DeepSeek-
R1-0528-Thinking and Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking, deliberately selected to span the full
agreement spectrum, from near-perfect consensus to extreme divergence. Because many
WritingBench tasks are in Chinese, some evaluators produced Chinese-language reasoning;
we present these verbatim and provide English translations (marked “translated from
Chinese original”).
E.1 Case A: Agreement on Quality
Task.Commercial copywriting for a high-end redwood furniture product detail page.
MERG Scores.Claude: 8.28, Gemini: 8.35, GPT: 8.40 (spread = 0.12).
19

Preprint.
Analysis.All three evaluators independently identified the same strengths and weak-
nesses after knowledge activation.
•Claude: “This copy achieves strong performance across all dimensions, with particular
excellence in sensory persuasion and material expertise. The brand narrative integration
is effective though incomplete (missing sub-brand differentiation).”
•Gemini: “Exceptional in its literary and narrative craft but flawed in its functional
execution. The scores for ‘Persuasive Brand Narrative’ (9.5) and ‘Structural Craftsmanship’
(9.5) are near-perfect [...] The lowest score, ‘Creative Problem-Solving’ (4.0), is a direct
consequence of the writer’s failure to resolve the central mahogany/redwood ambiguity.”
•GPT: “The most impressive aspect is the detailed, credible depiction of materials and
craftsmanship, which is critical for high-end redwood furniture [...] Brand voice is also
robust: it consistently communicates heritage, mastery, and exclusivity while avoiding
crass sales language.”
Baseline Comparison.At t=0.0, baseline evaluators also gave high scores (Brand Integra-
tion: 9.3, Persuasive Language: 9.3), but notably failed to penalize technical inaccuracies in
material claims (Technical Accuracy: only 4.7 in baseline). MERG’s knowledge activation
forced all three evaluators to independently discover and weight this deficiency,yet they
still convergedon a final score, demonstrating that agreement under knowledge grounding
reflects genuine consensus rather than shared heuristics.
E.2 Case B: Agreement on Failure
Task.Financial analysis report on a major gaming company’s overseas market perfor-
mance.
MERG Scores.Claude: 4.53, Gemini: 4.60, GPT: 4.68 (spread = 0.15).
Analysis.All three evaluators independently identified systematic data fabrication as a
critical failure.
•Claude: “This response represents a significant failure in financial analysis integrity
despite its professional appearance. While it accurately cites verifiable figures from the
source document (Q4 revenue, growth rates, mobile proportions, 蛋仔派对 metrics), it
extensively fabricates overseas data that the source explicitly does not contain.”
•Gemini: “The fatal issue is the systemic data fabrication, which renders the analysis
untrustworthy. This rightly results in a very low score (3.0) in the two most heavily
weighted dimensions: ‘Accuracy & Fidelity’ (50%) and ‘Analytical Integrity’ (25%).”
•GPT(translated from Chinese original): “ 在任务完成度、结构清晰度和语言表达方面
表现明显高于平均水准，完全可以达到 "Strong"级别 [...]然而根据本次 rubric的硬约
束， "财务数据准确性 "与"分析深度与不确定性管理 "是关键维度：文章在这些方面存在系
统问题 ——大量具体海外营收占比、区域结构，以及单款游戏海外 ARPPU/DAU/ 营收
贡献的数字是捏造的，且未做充分的推断标注或来源说明。 ” (Performance in task comple-
tion, structural clarity, and language expression is clearly above average, fully reaching “Strong”
level [...] However, per the rubric’s hard constraints, “Financial Data Accuracy” and “Analytical
Depth & Uncertainty Management” are key dimensions: the article has systematic problems:
detailed overseas revenue shares, regional structures, and per-game ARPPU/DAU/revenue figures
are fabricated without adequate inference labeling or source attribution.)
Interpretation.This case demonstrates that MERG enables evaluators to converge on
substantivefailures. In the baseline evaluation (Financial Data Accuracy: 5.0), the fabrication
issue was partially masked by the report’s professional formatting. Knowledge activation
forced all three evaluators to verify claims against domain knowledge, independently
reaching the same conclusion.
20

Preprint.
E.3 Case C: Knowledge-Driven Disagreement
Task.Patent disclosure document (专利交底书) based on an academic paper.
MERG Scores.Claude: 6.43, Gemini: 10.0, GPT: 3.2 (spread = 6.80).
Analysis.This extreme disagreement reveals fundamentally different evaluation philoso-
phies activated by domain knowledge.
•Gemini (10.0): Evaluated the document purely onformat and rhetorical execution: “For
‘Technical Depth’, the most heavily weighted dimension, the response provided a wealth
of invented, credible, and specific details that perfectly match the ‘Exceptional’ anchor
[...] its ‘Structural & Linguistic Compliance’ was perfect.” Gemini’s knowledge activation
focused on patent documentstructurerather thansubstance.
•Claude (6.43): Balanced format against content: “A document that successfully follows
patent disclosure conventions and makes reasonable attempts at innovation extraction,
but is undermined by significant technical accuracy issues [...] primarily due to the critical
misrepresentation of ZKP as an encryption mechanism rather than a proof system.”
•GPT (3.2)(translated from Chinese original): “ 技术正确性受到 ZKP概念误用和部分随
意参数设定的明显拖累，难以给高分 [...]在专利文体和结构方面则表现较好 [...]总体评
价略高于 "中等水平 "，视为质量中等偏上但存在明显瑕疵的初稿，给出约 3.2左右的综合
分。 ” (Technical correctness is significantly dragged down by ZKP concept misuse and arbitrary
parameter settings, making a high score difficult [...] patent style and structure perform well [...]
Overall slightly above “medium,” considered a draft of above-average quality but with obvious
flaws, yielding a composite score of approximately 3.2.)
Baseline Comparison.At t=0.0, baseline scores showed high agreement (Patent Structure:
8.0, Content Extraction: 7.7). The baseline consensus masked a fundamental question that
MERG exposed:should a patent disclosure be evaluated on its formal completeness or its legal
viability?Different evaluators, armed with the same domain knowledge, reached different
but individually defensible conclusions, a case of irreducible evaluative pluralism.
E.4 Case D: The Formatting Trap
Task.Series A investment pitch deck for an EdTech startup in China (post-2021).
MERG Scores.Claude: 6.48, Gemini: 9.9, GPT: 3.7 (spread = 6.20).
Analysis.This case (also illustrated in Figure 1) exemplifies the core Evaluation Illusion
phenomenon:
•Gemini (9.9): “Demonstrates a masterful understanding of the core task: creating a
persuasive investment pitch within a complex regulatory environment. It scores perfectly
on ‘Strategic Viability’ by turning policy constraints into a competitive advantage [...] This
is a benchmark example of an ‘Exceptional’ response.” Gemini activated knowledge about
pitch deck conventions but did not fully engage with China’s 2021 “Double Reduction”
policy implications.
•Claude (6.48)(translated from Chinese original): “ 内容层面存在几个关键缺陷： 1）完
全忽略双减政策，这是评估中国 EdTech投资的必要背景，文档虽将公司定位为职业教
育但未解释其政策安全性； 2）多处使用占位符（专利号、省份名称）而非真实数据，
表明这更像是模板而非真实案例； 3）财务预测存在内部矛盾（ ARPU计算值与声称值
不符）； 4）竞争分析缺乏客观性和可验证性。 ” (Several key flaws at the content level: 1)
completely ignores the Double Reduction policy, a necessary context for evaluating Chinese EdTech
investment. Though the document positions the company in vocational education, it does not
explain its policy safety; 2) multiple placeholders (patent numbers, province names) instead of
real data suggest this is more template than genuine case; 3) financial projections contain internal
contradictions (ARPU calculations don’t match claimed values); 4) competitive analysis lacks
objectivity and verifiability.)
21

Preprint.
•GPT (3.7)(translated from Chinese original): “ 项目描述为中国职业教育平台，却完全
不回应 "双减 "等关键监管，对在线办学资质等合规路径无交代 [...]财务部分虽然给出两
年预测，却没有按业务线拆分收入，用户数、 ARPU、企业客户之间逻辑混乱，单位经
济缺席，属于典型 "堆数字而不建模型 " [...]材料总体更接近 "故事化宣传稿 "，不足以支
撑严肃投资判断，因此将最终综合评价压在 4分以下，定为 3.7，以反映结构亮点与内
容不足之间的不平衡。 ” (The project is described as a Chinese vocational education platform
but completely ignores key regulations like the “Double Reduction” policy, with no explanation
of online education licensing compliance [...] Financials give a two-year projection but lack
per-business-line revenue splits, with confused logic among user counts, ARPU, and enterprise
clients, typical “stacking numbers without building models” [...] Overall closer to a “story-based
promotional piece,” insufficient for serious investment decisions, hence the final composite score is
set below 4 at 3.7, reflecting the imbalance between structural highlights and content deficiencies.)
Baseline Comparison.Baseline evaluators gave uniformly high scores (Strategic Position-
ing: 8.7, Content & Professionalism: 8.7, Financial Credibility: 7.3). The baseline consensus
was built entirely on surface-level professionalism. MERG deconstructed this consensus by
activating regulatory knowledge, revealing that evaluators weight domain violations very
differently, from Gemini’s format-first approach to GPT’s substance-first stance.
E.5 Case E: Cross-Model Consistency
Task.Professional film criticism essay comparing two Chinese TV crime dramas.
MERG Scores.Claude: 6.10, Gemini: 5.90, GPT: 5.80 (spread = 0.30).
Analysis.All evaluators converged on a “competent but flawed” assessment, indepen-
dently identifying the same critical error:
•Claude(translated from Chinese original): “ 文章在结构组织和叙事分析上表现尚可，展
现了一定的专业素养和比较视野。但存在几个关键问题： 1）将刘华强误写为 ’李天柱 ’是
重大事实错误，严重影响专业可信度； 2）分析缺乏具体场景支撑，多为概括性描述，
华丽修辞背后实质内容有限； 3）篇幅未达要求，两剧分析失衡； 4）结论缺乏更深层的
类型片发展洞见。综合来看，这是一篇中等水平的影视评论，有基本框架但深度不足，
有专业意识但执行有缺陷。 ” (The essay performs adequately in structural organization and
narrative analysis, demonstrating a degree of professional awareness and comparative perspective.
However, several key issues exist: 1) misidentifying Liu Huaqiang as “Li Tianzhu” is a major
factual error that severely undermines professional credibility; 2) analysis lacks specific scene
support, mostly consisting of generalizations with limited substance behind ornate rhetoric; 3)
length falls short of requirements with imbalanced coverage of the two dramas; 4) conclusions
lack deeper insights into crime genre development. Overall, a mid-level film critique with basic
structure but insufficient depth, with professional awareness but flawed execution.)
•Gemini: “Its mastery of academic structure, sophisticated language, and analytical
method is exceptional, earning scores of 9.0 and 9.5 in those dimensions. On the other
hand, it fails the most critical litmus test of the assignment, namely basic factual accuracy
about a main character [...] making it a high-quality failure.”
•GPT(translated from Chinese original): “ 各维度得分集中在 5–6区间，表明这是一个 "合
格但未出众 "的回应。比较结构清楚，叙事与人物分析方向正确 [...]然而，文章的专业性
更多体现在语言和宏观判断上，缺乏与之匹配的细节证据和具体分析：叙事技法、人物
弧光、关系网络、类型突破等论点少有具体场景支撑，未能体现 rubric 7–8分或以上所
要求的 "扎实例证 +机制拆解 "。” (Scores cluster in the 5 to 6 range, indicating “adequate but
not outstanding.” Comparative structure is clear, with correct direction in narrative and character
analysis [...] However, the article’s professionalism is more reflected in language and macro-level
judgments, lacking matching detailed evidence and specific analysis: arguments on narrative
technique, character arcs, relationship networks, and genre innovation lack specific scene support,
failing to meet the rubric’s 7 to 8+ requirement of “solid evidence + mechanism analysis.”)
Interpretation.This case demonstrates MERG’s capacity to producecalibratedagreement
even in subjective domains. Despite film criticism being inherently subjective (the Litera-

Figure 6:System validity: the three-tier quality hierarchy is robust.Score distributions for all
32 models across 3 evaluators at t=0.0. Despite the disagreement at the sample level documented
throughout this paper, evaluatorsunanimouslyrecover the Base <Instruct <Thinking quality ordering
(Cohen’s d= 2.58). This confirms that Evaluation Illusion does not invalidate LLM evaluation for
coarse-grained model comparison; it specifically affects the fine-grained, per-sample signals that
RLAIF reward models require.
ture domain shows ∆K=− 0.06 in Figure 4), knowledge activation anchored evaluators
on verifiable criteria (factual accuracy and textual evidence) while preserving legitimate
divergence on aesthetic judgments.
E.6 Summary of Patterns
Across these cases, three patterns emerge.
1.Knowledge-grounded agreement is more meaningful. When evaluators agree after
MERG (Cases A, B, E), they cite the same specific evidence (material accuracy, data
fabrication, factual errors) rather than generic praise for “professional tone” or “clear
structure.”
2.Disagreement reveals evaluation philosophy. When evaluators disagree after MERG
(Cases C, D), the disagreement isinterpretable, reflecting different but defensible weight-
ings of format vs. substance, structural completeness vs. legal validity.
3.Baseline agreement is shallow. In every case, baseline scores showed higher agree-
ment than MERG scores, but this agreement was anchored on surface features that all
evaluators could assess without domain expertise.
23

Preprint.
F Score Distributions
Figure 6 displays the score distributions for all 32 models across 3 evaluators at t=0.0,
providing the most comprehensive view of the evaluation landscape.
Three-Tier Quality Hierarchy.The most striking feature is the robust separation of model
tiers. Base models cluster around ¯s= 3.0 to 5.5, Instruct models around ¯s= 5.0 to 7.0,
and Thinking models around ¯s=5.5 to 7.5. This ordering is recoveredunanimouslyby all
three evaluators (Cohen’s d= 2.58 for Base vs. Thinking), confirming that LLM evaluation
remains valid for coarse-grained model comparison.
Score Compression in the High-Quality Region.Within the Thinking tier, score distribu-
tions become notably compressed, with the gap between the best and worst Thinking model
being only ∼1.5 points (compared to ∼3.5 points within Base models). This compression is
precisely the region where RLAIF reward models must discriminate between “good” and
“great,” and where Evaluation Illusion is most damaging. The narrow dynamic range means
that even small heuristic biases can invert model rankings at the sample level.
Evaluator-Specific Distribution Shapes.Across all models, evaluator-specific patterns
are visible.
•Gemini(green) consistently produces the most right-skewed distributions, with a ten-
dency toward high scores and less variance. This confirms the leniency bias documented
in our main experiments. Gemini’s distributions are shifted ∼0.5 to 1.0 points higher than
Claude and GPT across all model tiers.
•GPT(blue) shows the widest variance, with heavier tails in both directions. This suggests
GPT applies more differentiated scoring, producing stronger penalties for poor outputs
and higher rewards for excellent ones.
•Claude(red) occupies a middle ground, with distributions that most closely approximate
a normal shape. Claude’s scores are the most calibrated in terms of centering, though it
shares GPT’s tendency to penalize specific failures more aggressively than Gemini.
Model-Size Effects.Within each tier, larger models generally receive higher mean scores,
but the effect is modest ( ∆¯s≈0.3 to 0.5 per order of magnitude in parameter count). The
most notable exception is among Thinking models, where DeepSeek-R1-0528-Thinking and
Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking achieve the highest absolute scores despite also exhibiting the
lowest sample-level agreement (Figure 5), providing further evidence that the evaluation
system is simultaneouslyvalid at the model levelandunreliable at the sample level.
Implications for RLAIF.The combination of (1) compressed score ranges in the high-
quality tier, (2) evaluator-specific scoring biases, and (3) low sample-level agreement creates
a perfect storm for reward overoptimization. An RLAIF system training on these scores
would receive reward signals that are dominated by evaluator idiosyncrasies rather than
genuine quality differences, which is precisely the mechanism discussed in §4 (Implications
for Reward Modeling).
24

structure.title: Preface
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the limitations of using Large Language Models (LLMs) as evaluators, specifically the phenomenon of "Evaluation Illusion" where LLM judges generate sophisticated critiques but base their actual scores on shared surface heuristics. The document presents a study that challenges the assumption that high inter-evaluator agreement indicates reliable and objective evaluation, and introduces a framework called MERG (Metacognitive Enhanced Rubric Generation) that dynamically generates evaluation rubrics grounded in domain knowledge. The study finds that MERG reduces inter-evaluator agreement, indicating that baseline agreement is largely heuristic-driven, and that knowledge-grounded rubric generation produces more substantive evaluation. The document also discusses the implications of these findings for reward modeling and alignment pipelines, and highlights the importance of distinguishing between structural and substantive consensus in evaluation.

nodes.title: Introduction
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the limitations of using Large Language Models (LLMs) as judges for evaluating text quality, challenging the assumption that high inter-evaluator agreement indicates reliable and objective evaluation. The authors introduce the concept of "Evaluation Illusion," where LLM judges generate sophisticated critiques but anchor scores on shared surface heuristics rather than substantive quality. They propose a framework called MERG (Metacognitive Enhanced Rubric Generation) to dynamically generate evaluation rubrics grounded in domain knowledge, which increases agreement in codified domains and decreases it in subjective domains. The study reveals that baseline consensus is often heuristic-driven, and that knowledge injection can deconstruct the "Shared Illusion" and produce more meaningful assessments. The findings have implications for reward modeling in Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback (RLAIF) and suggest that evaluation rubrics should be dynamically enriched with expert knowledge rather than relying on generic criteria.

nodes.title: Methodology
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of "Evaluation Illusion" in the context of language models and their ability to evaluate the quality of written texts. The main points covered include the introduction of the MERG framework, which aims to mitigate the issue of evaluation illusion by incorporating knowledge grounding into the evaluation process. The document also presents an experiment that evaluates 32 large language models across three capability tiers, using a dataset of 100 prompts from the WritingBench platform. The results highlight the existence of a "Shared Illusion" among evaluators, where they unanimously reward professionally formatted texts despite their potential flaws, and demonstrate how the MERG framework can help reveal these flaws and provide more accurate evaluations. Additionally, the document touches on the "Rubric Commensurability Problem" and the "Resolution Paradox" as structural inflation mechanisms that contribute to the persistence of evaluation illusion.

nodes.title: Experimental Design
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of "Evaluation Illusion" in the context of language models and their evaluation. The main points covered include the introduction of the concept, an example of a pitch deck for an EdTech startup in China, and the evaluation of this pitch deck by different language models, including Claude 4.5 Opus and Gemini 2.5 Pro. The document highlights how these models can form a "Shared Illusion" by rewarding professional formatting while ignoring fundamental flaws in the business model. The introduction of MERG (Metacognitive Enhanced Rubric Generation) is also discussed as a framework for knowledge-grounded rubric generation to mitigate this illusion. The methodology section describes the experimental design, data, models, and metrics used to evaluate the language models, including the use of 32 models, three evaluators, and various metrics to measure agreement. Overall, the document explores the limitations of current evaluation methods and proposes a new approach to improve the evaluation of language models.

nodes.title: Metacognitive Enhanced Rubric Generation
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of "Evaluation Illusion" in the context of language model evaluation. It describes a three-tier design for evaluating language models, using three frontier LLMs as evaluators, and measures agreement at three granularities: sample-level, cell-level, and model-level. The document introduces a framework called Metacognitive Enhanced Rubric Generation (MERG) to test whether baseline evaluator agreement reflects genuine deliberation or heuristic-driven Evaluation Illusion. MERG is a four-stage framework that forces LLM evaluators to transition from heuristic-driven pattern matching to knowledge-grounded deliberation before assigning a score. The results show that deploying MERG's knowledge-grounded evaluation pipeline systematically reduces inter-evaluator agreement, providing empirical evidence for the presence of a Shared Illusion. The document also presents ablation variants to disentangle the sources of evaluator agreement and discusses the implications of the findings.

nodes.title: Results
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the comparison of evaluation paradigms, specifically the MERG method, which requires explicit metacognitive grounding. It highlights the differences between MERG and other methods, such as Direct Score, G-Eval, Prometheus, and WritingBench, in terms of knowledge grounding and rubric usage. The document also describes the stages of the MERG evaluation process, including dynamic rubric generation and calibrated evaluation. Additionally, it presents the results of an experiment that demonstrates the effectiveness of MERG in reducing inter-evaluator agreement, providing evidence for the presence of a "Shared Illusion" in evaluation paradigms. The document also introduces ablation variants to disentangle the sources of evaluator agreement, applied to two representative models, and interprets the results as empirical evidence for the robust and practically significant phenomenon of the Shared Illusion.

nodes.title: Deconstructing the Shared Illusion
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the comparison of evaluation paradigms, specifically the MERG (Metacognitive Evaluation and Rubric Generation) method, which requires explicit metacognitive grounding, unlike standard methods that rely on implicit knowledge and shared rubrics. The document presents tables and figures that illustrate the reduction of inter-evaluator agreement when using MERG, indicating the presence of a "Shared Illusion" where evaluators tend to converge on surface heuristics rather than substantive deliberation. The results show that MERG forces engagement with substantive, domain-specific criteria, mitigating heuristic consensus and revealing genuine disagreements. The document also discusses ablation variants, domain selectivity, and the relationship between output quality and inter-evaluator agreement, providing evidence for the "Evaluation Illusion" hypothesis.

nodes.title: The Resolution Paradox
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of "Evaluation Illusion" in the context of large language models (LLMs) and their evaluation. The main points covered include the idea that baseline agreement among evaluators is heavily reliant on shared surface heuristics rather than substantive deliberation, and that knowledge injection can systematically reduce inter-evaluator agreement. The document presents evidence from experiments, including tables and figures, to support the claim that evaluators tend to agree on coarse distinctions but exhibit illusory agreement or disagree substantially on fine-grained judgments within a high-quality tier. The "Resolution Paradox" is introduced, which defines the operational limit of LLM evaluators and suggests that while they are reliable for model-level benchmarking, their per-sample signals are heavily contaminated by Evaluation Illusion.

nodes.title: The Rubric Commensurability Problem
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the evaluation of language models, specifically the agreement between human evaluators on the quality of model outputs. The main points covered include the existence of a "Resolution Paradox" where evaluators agree on the overall quality of models but disagree on individual outputs, and the "Rubric Commensurability Problem" where the structure of evaluation rubrics heavily influences evaluator agreement. The document also presents results from an experiment using a knowledge-grounded rubric generation method called MERG, which shows that the majority of reported inter-evaluator agreement is artificially scaffolded by standardized evaluation instruments. Additionally, the document highlights domain-selective effects on evaluator agreement, where knowledge activation increases agreement in codified domains such as education and academic fields, but decreases agreement in subjective domains like literature. Overall, the document suggests that evaluator agreement is not always a reliable measure of model quality and that a more nuanced understanding of evaluation methodologies is needed.

nodes.title: Domain-Dependent Knowledge Activation
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of evaluator agreement and how it can be influenced by the structure of evaluation instruments, such as rubrics. The main points covered include the idea that a significant portion of reported inter-evaluator agreement is not a genuine convergence in judgment, but rather a shared illusion artificially scaffolded by standardized evaluation instruments. The document also explores the concept of domain-selective knowledge effects, where knowledge-grounded rubric generation produces different effects on evaluator agreement in various domains, such as codified domains (e.g., Education, Academic) and subjective domains (e.g., Literature). Additionally, the document touches on language-dependent knowledge activation quality and provides qualitative evidence of the effectiveness of the proposed approach, including examples of deep disagreements between evaluators with different levels of domain expertise.

nodes.title: Discussion
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of "Evaluation Illusion" in the context of large language models (LLMs) and their evaluation. The main points covered include the fragility of consensus among LLM evaluators, the importance of domain-specific knowledge in evaluation, and the need for dynamic and knowledge-driven evaluation rubrics. The document highlights the limitations of traditional evaluation methods, which often rely on surface-level heuristics and shared instruments, and proposes a new approach called MERG, which injects domain knowledge into the evaluation process. The results show that MERG can lead to more substantive evaluation, but also decreases agreement among evaluators, particularly in subjective domains. The document also discusses the implications of these findings for reward modeling, evaluation pipeline design, and the development of more effective evaluation methods.

nodes.title: Related Work
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of "Evaluation Illusion" in the context of Large Language Models (LLMs) and their evaluation. The main points covered include the fragility of consensus among LLM evaluators, the distinction between structural and substantive agreement, and the potential for evaluation rubrics to be dynamically generated with explicit domain knowledge injection. The document also explores the implications of Evaluation Illusion for reward modeling, highlighting the potential for reward signals to be flawed and leading to overoptimization. Additionally, the document discusses related work in LLM evaluation, rubric-based evaluation, and metacognition, and provides practical recommendations for auditing agreement depth, matching depth to domain, and diversifying evaluation signals. Overall, the document argues that Evaluation Illusion is a significant problem in LLM evaluation and proposes a new approach to evaluation that prioritizes substantive agreement and knowledge-driven evaluation rubrics.

nodes.title: Conclusion
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the limitations of using Large Language Models (LLMs) as evaluators, specifically the phenomenon of "Evaluation Illusion" where LLM judges output sophisticated critiques but base their actual scores on shared surface heuristics. The document highlights the importance of task-relevant expert knowledge in evaluation and introduces the concept of dynamically generating evaluation rubrics grounded in domain knowledge. It also discusses the limitations of current evaluation methods, including the use of static and generic rubrics, and the potential risks of over-relying on automated evaluation for alignment. The document presents findings from experiments that demonstrate the effectiveness of knowledge-grounded rubric generation in producing more calibrated assessments and suggests a constructive path forward for the alignment pipeline.

nodes.title: Limitations
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the limitations of Large Language Model (LLM) evaluation systems, specifically the "Evaluation Illusion" where models agree on surface-level features but not on substantive aspects. The authors demonstrate that dynamically generating evaluation rubrics grounded in domain knowledge can produce more calibrated assessments. They also highlight the "Resolution Paradox" and the "Rubric Commensurability Problem", which suggest that LLM evaluation is valid but shallow. The document proposes a constructive path forward, recommending the use of knowledge-grounded rubric generation and distinguishing between structural and substantive consensus. The study has several limitations, including the lack of human ground-truth annotations and the limited generalizability to exact-answer domains. The authors emphasize the importance of responsible AI development and encourage more rigorous evaluation practices to mitigate the risks of over-relying on automated evaluation.

nodes.title: Ethics Statement
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the limitations of Large Language Model (LLM) evaluation systems, specifically the "Evaluation Illusion" where models appear to agree on evaluations but are actually relying on surface-level heuristics rather than substantive understanding. The document presents research demonstrating that dynamically generated evaluation rubrics grounded in domain knowledge can improve the calibration of assessments and mitigate the Evaluation Illusion. The study highlights the importance of distinguishing between structural and substantive consensus and recommends adopting knowledge-grounded rubric generation as a standard practice in evaluation pipelines. The document also discusses the limitations of the study, including the lack of human ground-truth annotations and the limited generalizability of the findings to certain domains and models. Overall, the document suggests that LLM evaluation is valid but shallow and requires more rigorous evaluation practices to ensure reliability and accuracy.

Overall, the partial document suggests a focus on the evaluation and improvement of LLMs, with an emphasis on developing more effective and efficient methods for assessing their performance.
