structure.title: MERG Prompt Templates
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the evaluation of language models using a framework called MERG, which consists of four stages: Knowledge Activation, Metacognitive Reflection, Dynamic Rubric Generation, and Calibrated Evaluation. The document also presents rankings of 32 models based on their performance, with the top-ranked models showing the lowest evaluator agreement, confirming the Evaluation Illusion hypothesis. Additionally, the document includes case studies that illustrate distinct patterns of Evaluation Illusion, highlighting the importance of careful evaluation and bias awareness when assessing language model performance. The main points covered in the partial document include the MERG evaluation framework, model rankings, and case studies demonstrating the Evaluation Illusion hypothesis.

structure.title: Full Model Rankings
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the rankings of 32 models based on their scores and agreement among evaluators. The top-ranked models, categorized as "Thinking" tier, show the lowest agreement among evaluators, confirming the "Evaluation Illusion" hypothesis. The rankings reveal a clear pattern, with "Base" models occupying the bottom tier and showing high agreement. The document also presents case studies illustrating distinct patterns of Evaluation Illusion, including an analysis of a patent disclosure sample where different evaluators (Gemini, Claude, and GPT) provided varying scores and feedback. The main points covered include model rankings, evaluation agreement, and the Evaluation Illusion hypothesis, with a focus on the differences in scoring and feedback among various models and evaluators.

structure.title: Extended Case Studies
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the evaluation of 32 models using a scoring system, with a focus on the "Evaluation Illusion" hypothesis. The main points covered include the ranking of models based on their scores and agreement among evaluators, with top-quality models showing the lowest agreement. The document also presents case studies illustrating distinct patterns of Evaluation Illusion, where evaluators disagree on the quality of model outputs due to differences in their scoring heuristics. Additionally, the document analyzes the sample-level agreement landscape across all 32 models at various temperature settings, revealing a consistent hierarchy of agreement among evaluators and an inverse relationship between model quality and evaluator agreement. The findings suggest that the Evaluation Illusion is a structural property of how evaluators process writing quality, rather than a consequence of sampling noise.

structure.title: Sample-Level Agreement
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the evaluation of language models, specifically Gemini, Claude, and GPT, in terms of their ability to assess writing quality. It presents several case studies that highlight the differences in evaluation approaches between the models, including the tendency of Gemini to reward surface-level presentation over substance. The document also explores the concept of "Evaluation Illusion" and the "Resolution Paradox," which refer to the discrepancy between model-level and sample-level agreement among evaluators. Additionally, it examines the impact of temperature settings on sample-level agreement and presents a comprehensive view of the sample-level agreement landscape across various models and temperature settings. The main points covered include the susceptibility of Gemini to Evaluation Illusion, the differences in evaluation heuristics between Claude and GPT, and the inverse relationship between model quality and evaluator agreement.

structure.title: Case Studies Under MERG
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the evaluation of large language models (LLMs) using a method called MERG, which activates domain knowledge in evaluators to assess the quality of LLM responses. The document presents several case studies that illustrate how MERG reshapes evaluator behavior, leading to more informed and nuanced evaluations. The cases cover various tasks, including commercial copywriting, financial analysis, patent disclosure, investment pitch decks, and film criticism. The results show that MERG enables evaluators to converge on substantive failures and strengths, even in subjective domains, and exposes fundamental differences in evaluation philosophies. The document also highlights the importance of considering domain knowledge and context in evaluating LLM responses, and demonstrates the potential of MERG to produce calibrated agreement among evaluators.

nodes.title: Case E: Cross-Model Consistency
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the evaluation of various projects and texts by different models, including Claude, Gemini, and GPT. The main points covered include the importance of knowledge-grounded agreement in evaluation, the differences in evaluation philosophy among models, and the limitations of baseline agreement. The document also highlights the need for evaluators to consider specific evidence and verifiable criteria when assessing texts, rather than relying on surface-level features or generic praise. Additionally, the document touches on the concept of "Evaluation Illusion" and its impact on fine-grained model comparison. Overall, the partial document explores the complexities of evaluation and the importance of nuanced and informed assessment in various domains.

nodes.title: Summary of Patterns
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the evaluation of large language models (LLMs) and the challenges associated with it. The main points covered include the existence of an "Evaluation Illusion" that affects fine-grained model comparison, the importance of knowledge-grounded agreement among evaluators, and the emergence of three patterns in evaluation: knowledge-grounded agreement, disagreement revealing evaluation philosophy, and baseline agreement being shallow. The document also presents the results of an evaluation experiment involving 32 models and three evaluators, which shows a robust three-tier quality hierarchy and highlights the issues of score compression, evaluator-specific biases, and model-size effects. The implications of these findings for reward modeling and the development of RLAIF systems are also discussed.

structure.title: Score Distributions
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the evaluation of 32 models across three evaluators, revealing a three-tier quality hierarchy with distinct score distributions for Base, Instruct, and Thinking models. The document highlights the compression of score ranges in the high-quality tier, evaluator-specific scoring biases, and low sample-level agreement, which can lead to reward overoptimization in RLAIF systems. The main points covered include the separation of model tiers, score compression, evaluator-specific distribution shapes, model-size effects, and the implications of these findings for RLAIF and reward modeling.
